content: Epilepsy diagnosis requires: (1) Detailed history from patient and witnesses describing event semiology (onset, progression, duration, postictal state). Differentiate from syncope, psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES), movement disorders. (2) EEG: interictal epileptiform discharges (spikes, sharp waves) support diagnosis but normal EEG doesn't exclude epilepsy. Sleep-deprived or prolonged EEG increases yield. Ictal recording confirms diagnosis and localizes onset. Video-EEG monitoring is gold standard for refractory cases. (3) Brain MRI with epilepsy protocol (thin-cut coronal images through hippocampi) to identify structural causes: mesial temporal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, tumors, vascular malformations, hippocampal sclerosis. (4) Laboratory: electrolytes, glucose, toxicology, AED levels if applicable. (5) Consider genetic testing for specific syndromes (e.g., SCN1A in Dravet syndrome). ILAE classification guides treatment: focal vs generalized onset determines AED choice. PNES diagnosis requires video-EEG demonstrating typical events without electrographic correlate.
